articles.title: Zork name origin got an update on Wikipedia
articles.summary: Solving the "Zork" mystery has been ongoing on Wikipedia after two years of restoration efforts by the user who restored their last save in May 2026. The issue was addressed through a thorough examination of Zork's trivia and a search for additional sources to support the claim that "zork" referred to an unfinished program in MIT Dynamic Modeling Group back in the 1970s. This led to a contradiction between different sources, including those from Marc Blank and Tim Anderson, who were both involved in creating the game. The discrepancy was finally resolved when the user searched for other Zork-related documents and found a previously unknown article that clarified the origin of the name.

The company was told to cancel its membership in Elkjop's customer club and opt out of marketing emails, but instead, it found the problem by switching off the club altogether. This is an example of forced consent, where a company forces individuals to surrender their rights for free in order to exercise them, violating Article 21(2) of the GDPR and the ePrivacy Directive.

articles.title: The design of littlefs
articles.summary: The design of littlefs is centered around building a resilient filesystem that can survive power-loss and flash wear without using unbounded memory. Littlefs was originally built to learn about filesystem design in microcontrollers, where 32-bit devices like those used in embedded systems have limited resources. The system differs from others by handling erasing and programming operations differently, with programming being relatively cheap but requiring an expensive destructive operation that can be costly on small flash storage.

articles.title: The Token Compression Illusion: Why I'm Skeptical of RTK
articles.summary: RTK's pitch sounds like an absolute developer cheat code that promises significant savings without delivering on its claims, with the industry already buying into the hype due to its 60k GitHub stars and potential cost savings. However, a closer look reveals critical structural flaws in how RTK compresses terminal output for LLM agents, including the tool's failure to accurately parse and interpret text, leading to inaccurate metrics such as Task Success Rate. This raises concerns about the long-term viability and operational safety of RTK, particularly when it comes to its reliance on a brittle external layer to optimize CLI tools without compromising semantic meaning.

articles.title: offset_of! slices
articles.summary: std::mem::offset_of! slices allows developers to compute the offset of a particular field in a struct at compile-time, taking into account the alignment and size of the containing type. This feature is particularly useful for dynamically sized types that do not implement Sized, such as Rust's dyn Debug trait. By supporting this behavior on unstable offset_of_slice feature, developers can leverage its capabilities to optimize their code and improve performance in certain scenarios.

articles.title: The Hidden Elegance of Gradient Noise
articles.summary: Perlin noise provides a way to generate structured pseudo-randomness that can be used to create natural-looking textures in graphics. This deterministic function has three properties: smooth transitions, stable output across frames, and isotropic appearance. Unlike simple grid of blurred random dots, Perlin noise is generated in three steps: first, it creates a tile space in the form of a grid with pseudo-random gradient vectors at every corner; second, it calculates how strongly each corner's gradient points toward any given point using dot products; third, it blends the contributions of surrounding corners.
